Attys. WARREN R. WEBSTER and ERIK S. FAUSNER of Hassard Bonnington (Tel: 415-288-9800) and ROBERT R. DEERING of J Supple Law (Tel: 415-366-5533) appearing for defendant David King-Stephens, M.D.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT DAVID KING-STEPHENS, M.D.S' MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT.

Matter argued and submitted by the parties. The Court adopts its tentative ruling:

Defendant David King-Stephens' motion for summary judgment or, in the alternative, summary adjudication of issues is denied in its entirety. Plaintiffs submitted sufficient admissible evidence to satisfy their summary burden of showing that no later than October 19, 2015 both plaintiffs suspected, and a reasonable person would have suspected, that Dr. King-Stephens' refusal earlier that year to order a brain scan (MRI) for Mr. Presley-Nelson was both wrongful and a substantial factor in causing harm to them. Plaintiffs submitted sufficient evidence to create triable issues whether either or both of them suspected, and whether a reasonable person would have suspected, wrongdoing by Dr. King-Stephens prior to March 15, 2016. Those facts are: 1) Ms. Presley-Nelson's declarations when she prepared the tumor history document and that the statements she wrote in that document did not reflect her state of mind as of the dates reflected in that document, but only her state of mind in November or December 2016; 2) Mr. Presley-Nelson's corrections to his deposition transcript; 3) Mr. Presley-Nelson's declaration stating, in substance, that he did not suspect and had no reason to suspect any wrongdoing by Dr. King-Stephens in connection with his 2015 diagnosis, surgery and treatment for a new brain tumor until some date after March 15, 2016; and 4) Ms. Presley-Nelson's deposition testimony stating, in substance, that she did not suspect and had no reason to suspect any wrongdoing by Dr. King-Stephens in connection with her husband's 2015 diagnosis, surgery and treatment for a new brain tumor until some date after March 15, 2016. Though I have serious doubts about the credibility of some or all of this evidence, it is not my province on a summary motion to evaluate witnesses' credibility. The apparently undisputed facts that Ms. Presley-Nelson asked Dr. King-Stephens to do a brain scan and Dr. King-Stephens refused, thereafter Mr. Presley-Nelson changed doctors and was informed by his new doctor after an MRI that he had a new brain tumor, Mr. King-Stephens requested his medical records for the period that Dr. King-Stephens was his doctor, and Mr. Presley-Nelson had some unspecified "questions" about his treatment by Dr. King-Stephens, all of which occurred by October 19, 2015, does not, in the face of the evidence submitted by the plaintiffs referred to previously, establish as a matter of law that the plaintiffs, or either of them, suspected or a reasonable person would have suspected, wrongdoing by Dr. King-Stephens prior to March 15, 2016, notwithstanding that all of this evidence is extremely probative. Liberally construing the evidence submitted by plaintiffs and strictly construing the evidence submitted by Dr. King-Stephens and resolving all doubts in favor of the plaintiffs as required by summary motion rules, the trier of fact, not a summary motion judge, must decide whether plaintiffs' claims are barred by CCP 340.5. (See, e.g., Rosas v. BASF Corporation (2015) 236 Cal. App. 4th 1378 (reversing trial court's grant of summary judgment notwithstanding highly probative undisputed evidence that the plaintiff suspected or should have suspected that wrongdoing by the defendant was a cause of the plaintiff's harm outside the limitations period)).

Plaintiffs are instructed to submit a proposed order to the court.

Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Maria A. Torreano CSR#8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/HK)

RULING: There being no appearance or objection, the tentative ruling is adopted on PLAINTIFFS GARY PRESLEY-NELSON, BEVERLY PRESLEY-NELSON's Motion To Seal, Or To Strike as follows:

Plaintiffs Gary and Beverly Presley-Nelson's motion to seal or to strike regarding assertedly privileged documents that were assertedly inadvertently produced by plaintiffs is denied in its entirety. Plaintiffs have not shown that the assertedly privileged documents are protected by either the attorney-client privilege or the attorney work product doctrine. Plaintiffs' request to seal or strike Mr. Presley-Nelson's birthdate is untimely since it was made for the first time in plaintiffs' reply papers and thus Dr. King-Stephens had no opportunity to oppose that request.

Counsel for plaintiff is to prepare and submit a proposed order that complies with CRC 3.1312.

RULING - A R G U E D. The Court announces it's modified tentative ruling as stated on the record. The motion to strike is granted as to everything except paragraph 45 which is taken off calendar in light of the tentative ruling on demurrer; with regard to attorney's fees and restitution - is taken off calendar instead of granted. Plaintiff argues. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT DAVID KING-STEPHENS M.D'S DEMURRER TO PLAINTIFF'S 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT

Defendant David King-Stephens' demurrer to the fourth and seventh causes of action for financial elder abuse and violation of the Unfair Competition Law in the first amended complaint filed by plaintiffs Gary R. Presley-Nelson and Beverly Presley-Nelson is sustained without leave to amend as to both causes of action. Mr. Presley-Nelson failed to sufficiently allege ultimate facts showing that Dr. King-Stephens took his money using undue influence or with an intent to defraud. While Mr. Presley-Nelson alleges Dr. King-Stephens took his money with knowledge about the likely harm resulting from incompetent health care services, the statute requires Mr. Presley-Nelson to show defendant's knowledge about harm resulting from the taking of plaintiff's property. Welfare & Instituitons. Code15610.30 (b). Mr. Presley-Nelson failed to sufficiently state with reasonable particularity the facts supporting the statutory elements of the UCL violation, here the alleged violation of the false advertising law. Mr. Presley-Nelson has instead alleged facts supporting a medical malpractice cause of action. Since Mr. Presley-Nelson has already been given leave to amend as to the fourth and seventh causes of action and failed to allege viable claims, it does not appear reasonably possible that he can do so.

Order is signed in Open Court. (see scanned order for details)
